revision.text:
{{short description|Japanese deity of agriculture}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ukanomitama-no-Kami | image = Statue of Ukanomitama at Ozu-jinja.jpg | caption = Heian period statue of Ukanomitama at [[:ja:小津神社|Ozu Shrine]] in [[Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama]], [[Shiga Prefecture]] | god_of = Goddess of food and agriculture | script_name = Japanese | script = 宇迦之御魂神 | other_names = Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto (倉稲魂命)
Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia09]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

{{short description|Japanese deity of agriculture}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ukanomitama-no-Kami | image = Statue of Ukanomitama at Ozu-jinja.jpg | caption = Heian period statue of Ukanomitama at [[:ja:小津神社|Ozu Shrine]] in [[Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama]], [[Shiga Prefecture]] | god_of = Goddess of food and agriculture | script_name = Japanese | script = 宇迦之御魂神 | other_names = Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto (倉稲魂命)
Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia11]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

{{short description|Japanese deity of agriculture}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ukanomitama-no-Kami | image = Statue of Ukanomitama at Ozu-jinja.jpg | caption = Heian period statue of Ukanomitama at [[:ja:小津神社|Ozu Shrine]] in [[Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama]], [[Shiga Prefecture]] | god_of = Goddess of food and agriculture | script_name = Japanese | script = 宇迦之御魂神 | other_names = Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto (倉稲魂命)
Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Food deities]] [[category:Agricultural gods]] [[category:Agricultural deities]] [[category:Inari faith]] [[category:Kunitsukami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Food deities]] [[category:Agricultural gods]] [[category:Agricultural deities]] [[category:Inari faith]] [[category:Kunitsukami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:qq]] [[category:ファイル]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Food deities]] [[category:Agricultural gods]] [[category:Agricultural deities]] [[category:Inari faith]] [[category:Kunitsukami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Food deities]] [[category:Agricultural gods]] [[category:Agricultural deities]] [[category:Inari faith]] [[category:Kunitsukami]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[category:mm]][[category:mm]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[category:mass bump]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Wikipedia]][[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

[https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 4]] [[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 4]] [[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 4]] [[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 4]] [[en:Ukanomitama]]]] [[Category:mm]][[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6 3]][[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ukanomitama]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Ukanomitama]][[ja:宇迦之御魂神]][[en:Ukanomitama]] {{Japan-myth-stub}}

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. {{short description|Japanese deity of agriculture}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ukanomitama-no-Kami | image = Statue of Ukanomitama at Ozu-jinja.jpg | caption = Heian period statue of Ukanomitama at [[:ja:小津神社|Ozu Shrine]] in [[Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama]], [[Shiga Prefecture]] | god_of = Goddess of food and agriculture | script_name = Japanese | script = 宇迦之御魂神 | other_names = Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto (倉稲魂命)
Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. {{short description|Japanese deity of agriculture}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ukanomitama-no-Kami | image = Statue of Ukanomitama at Ozu-jinja.jpg | caption = Heian period statue of Ukanomitama at [[:ja:小津神社|Ozu Shrine]] in [[Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama]], [[Shiga Prefecture]] | god_of = Goddess of food and agriculture | script_name = Japanese | script = 宇迦之御魂神 | other_names = Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto (倉稲魂命)
Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] {{short description|Japanese deity of agriculture}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Ukanomitama-no-Kami | image = Statue of Ukanomitama at Ozu-jinja.jpg | caption = Heian period statue of Ukanomitama at [[:ja:小津神社|Ozu Shrine]] in [[Moriyama, Shiga|Moriyama]], [[Shiga Prefecture]] | god_of = Goddess of food and agriculture | script_name = Japanese | script = 宇迦之御魂神 | other_names = Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto (倉稲魂命)
Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:Ukanomitama]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Ukanomitama]] [[en:Ukanomitama]] [[ja:宇迦之御魂神]]

Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ukanomitama]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Ukanomitama]] [[en:Ukanomitama]] [[ja:宇迦之御魂神]]

Mikura-no-Kami (御倉神)
Miketsukami (御食津神, 三狐神) | cult_center = [[Fushimi Inari Taisha]], [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort = | parents = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] and [[Kamuōichihime]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ukanomitama]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Ukanomitama]] [[en:Ukanomitama]] [[ja:宇迦之御魂神]]

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L. (2015). ''Kojiki''. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|978-1400878000}}. ==See also== *[[Toyouke-Ōmikami]] *[[Ukemochi]] {{Inari Faith}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Food deities]] [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Agricultural deities]] [[Category:Inari faith]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ukanomitama]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Ukanomitama]] [[en:Ukanomitama]] [[ja:宇迦之御魂神]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] {{translated page|ja|宇迦之御魂神|version=2325928|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi]] (''Kojiki'') | children = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and others }} '''Ukanomitama''' (宇迦之御魂神 – Mighty Soul of Sustenance{{Cite web|title=Ukanomitama • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/ukanomitama/|access-date=2021-09-11|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}} - ''[[Kojiki]]'') (倉稲魂命 - ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'') is a ''[[kami]]'' in classical [[Japanese mythology]], associated with food and agriculture, often identified with [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], the deity of rice. ==Name and mythology== The ''Kojiki'' identifies Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神 ''Ukanomitama-no-Kami'') as the child of [[Susanoo]] by his second wife Kamu-Ōichihime (神大市比売), who was a daughter of [[Kamiumi|Ōyamatsumi]] (大山津見神), the god of mountains. This text portrays Ukanomitama as the younger sibling of the harvest deity [[Toshigami|Ōtoshi-no-Kami]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-Of-The-Great Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=92}} A variant account recorded in the ''Nihon Shoki'' meanwhile portrays Ukanomitama (here referred to as 倉稲魂命 ''Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto'') as an offspring of [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] who were born when the two became hungry.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=22}} The deity's name is understood as being derived from ''uka no mitama'', "august spirit (''mitama'') of food (''uka'')".{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press|page=621}} While the above texts are silent regarding the deity's gender, Ukanomitama has long been interpreted to be female, perhaps due to association with other agricultural deities such as [[Toyouke-Ōmikami|Toyouke]] or [[Ukemochi]]. ==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book|last1=Aston|first1=William George, tr.|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|date=1896|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|location=London|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book|last1=Chamberlain|first1=Basil, tr.|title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters"|date=1882|publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co.|location=Yokohama|url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L.
